display_name: Eagle Teams Fare Enforcement on Buses
raw_text: On buses, we continue to scale and refine the deployment of our Eagle Teams to enforce fare payment onboard. Since this new deployment strategy was introduced in Q3 of 2024, fare evasion has gone down precipitously. Currently, Eagle Teams are interacting with roughly 1,500 buses every single day, with more Eagle Teams on the way later this year.

payload.description: Eagle Teams deployed to enforce fare payment onboard buses, interacting with roughly 1,500 buses per day

raw_text: At the same time, we are expanding modifications to infrastructure to limit the rate of gate evasion. By installing a delay to the emergency gates at three pilot stations in 2024, we were able to observe their effectiveness and map out a plan to expand this intervention in 2025. We have begun to scale this intervention up in recent months and have committed to have gate delays operational at 150 stations by the end of 2025.

payload.description: Installation of delays to emergency gates to limit gate evasion, committed to have gate delays operational at 150 stations by end of 2025

display_name: Unarmed Gate Guards at Exit Gates
raw_text: The exit gates have long been the superhighway of fare evasion. In the Blue-Ribbon Panel's report, it was estimated that roughly half of all fare evasion occurs through these gates. To stem this source of evasion, the MTA has been deploying unarmed guards at exit gates. Their presence reduces unnecessary usage of these gates and limits opportunities for evasion. Gate guards are currently deployed at over 200 stations

payload.description: Unarmed guards deployed at exit gates to reduce fare evasion

Conceptual offset bus lane alignment for Webster Avenue SBS. Advantages: easier for deliveries, possibility of neck downs and pedestrian islands, no loss of parking, people waiting for bus do not block sidewalk, better for buses than curbside, can add landscaping. Disadvantages: only one lane of traffic in each direction, conflicts between buses and cars as cars move across bus lane to park, right turns problematic.

Average stop spacing: approximately a 1/2-mile (2,640 feet).

raw_text: The purpose of Express routes is to connect neighborhoods in the outer boroughs to the central business district in Manhattan with a one-seat ride via the highway.

Average stop spacing: ~1/3 mile (1,760 feet) in local neighborhoods/Manhattan, excluding highway non-stop portion.

Connects outer borough neighborhoods to Manhattan central business district via highway. Uses coach buses, higher fare than Local. Shown in three colors: orange (Downtown), purple (Midtown), dark green (Midtown via Downtown). Mostly peak-hour service. Average stop spacing ~1/3 mile (1,760 feet) locally and in Manhattan, excluding highway non-stop portion.

Serves high-ridership, high-density corridors. Shown in red. Combines with Crosstown (SBS) routes to form high-frequency core network. Stops at high ridership locations and key transfer points. Frequent all-day (6AM-8PM weekdays). Average stop spacing ~1/3 mile or greater.

raw_text: The purpose of Limited routes is to serve high-ridership, high-density corridors and connect quickly across the city. They combine with Crosstown (SBS) routes to form a high-frequency core network. Shown in red. Average stop spacing: approximately 1/3 of a mile or greater. Frequent all day (between 6:00 AM and 8:00 PM on weekdays).

payload.description: Serves high-ridership, high-density corridors. Color: red. Average stop spacing: ~1/3 mile or greater. Frequent all day 6 AM-8 PM weekdays.

raw_text: The purpose of Local routes is to connect local neighborhoods, key transit hubs, and important destinations. They are shown in a green color. Service frequencies are typically driven by ridership demand. The average distance between stops on Local routes is approximately between 1,000 and 1,320 feet (1/4 mile).

payload.description: Connects local neighborhoods, key transit hubs, and important destinations. Color: green. Average stop spacing: 1,000-1,320 feet (1/4 mile).

Connects local neighborhoods, key transit hubs, and important destinations. Shown in green. Average stop spacing approximately between 1,000 and 1,320 feet (1/4 mile). Frequency driven by ridership demand.

raw_text: The purpose of Rush routes is to connect quickly between outer borough neighborhoods and subway stations. Shown in purple. These routes pick up locally and then travel as fast as possible to the subway, stopping only at major transfer points and key destinations. Average stop spacing: approximately 1/4 mile (1,320 feet) in local neighborhoods, approximately 1/3 mile or greater on the 'non-stop' portion.

payload.description: Connects quickly between outer borough neighborhoods and subway stations. Color: purple. Local stop spacing: ~1/4 mile. Non-stop portion spacing: ~1/3 mile or greater. More frequent in AM/PM weekday peak periods.

Connects quickly between outer borough neighborhoods and subway stations. Shown in purple. Picks up locally then travels non-stop to subway stopping only at major transfer points. More frequent in AM/PM peak periods. Average stop spacing ~1/4 mile locally, ~1/3 mile or greater on non-stop portion.
